subject: Physics
solution:
For any current loop or spinning charge distribution, the magnetic field at a far-away axial distance can be determined using its net magnetic dipole moment ($M_{\text{dipole}}$).

The magnetic field on the axis of a magnetic dipole at a far distance $z$ is

$$ B = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\frac{2M_{\text{dipole}}}{z^3} $$

Let the hollow cone have its tip at the origin $O(0,0,0)$ and its base of radius $R$ at a height $h$ along the $Z$-axis.

Let the slant length of the cone is

$$ L_{\text{slant}} = \sqrt{R^2 + h^2}. $$

If the semi-vertical angle $\alpha$ of the cone,

$$ \sin\alpha = \frac{R}{L_{\text{slant}}} = R\sqrt{R^2 + h^2} $$

Consider a small circular ring element on the curved surface of the cone at a slant distance $l$ from the origin $(0 \leq l \leq L_{\text{slant}})$ with a slant width $dl$.

The radius of this small ring element is

$$ r = l\sin\alpha $$

The vertical coordinate of this ring along the $Z$-axis is

$$ y = l\cos\alpha $$

The total curved surface area of the hollow cone is:

$$ A_{\text{total}} = \pi R L_{\text{slant}} $$

Since the total charge $Q$ is uniformly distributed over the curved surface, the surface charge density $\sigma$ is:

$$ \sigma = \frac{Q}{\pi R L_{\text{slant}}} $$

The surface area of the thin ring element of slant radius $r$ and slant width $dl$ is :

$\mathrm{dA}=2 \pi \mathrm{r} \cdot \mathrm{dl}=2 \pi(\mathrm{l} \sin \alpha) \mathrm{dl}$

Therefore, the infinitesimal charge $dq$ contained on this ring element is :

$\mathrm{dq}=\sigma \cdot \mathrm{dA}=\left(\mathrm{Q} \pi \mathrm{R} \mathrm{L}_{\text {slant }}\right) \cdot 2 \pi \mathrm{l} \sin \alpha \mathrm{dl}$

Substituting $\sin \alpha = \frac{R}{L_{\text{slant}}}$ into the equation :

$\mathrm{dq}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\pi \mathrm{R} \mathrm{L}_{\text {slant }}} \times 2 \pi \mathrm{l}\left(\mathrm{RL}_{\text {slant }}\right) \mathrm{dl}=\frac{2 \mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{L}_{\text {slant }}^2} \mathrm{ldl}$

When the cone rotates about its central Z-axis with an angular velocity $\omega$, the charge $dq$ on the ring element spins in a circular path of radius $r$, creating an effective small loop current $dI$:

$$dI = \frac{dq}{\text{Time period of one rotation}} = \frac{dq}{2\pi / \omega} = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} \, dq$$

The magnetic dipole moment $dM$ of a flat current loop carrying a current $dI$ enclosing an area $A_{\text{loop}} = \pi r^2$ is defined as:

$$dM = dI \cdot (\pi r^2)$$

Substituting $dI = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} \, dq$ and $r = l \sin \alpha$:

$$dM = \left(\frac{\omega}{2\pi} dq\right) \pi (l \sin \alpha)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \omega \sin^2 \alpha \times l^2 \times dq$$

$$dM = \frac{1}{2} \omega \sin^2 \alpha \times l^2 \times \left(\frac{2Q}{L_{\text{slant}}^2} l \, dl\right)$$

$$dM = \frac{Q\omega \sin^2 \alpha}{L_{\text{slant}}^2} \, l^3 \, dl$$

To find the total magnetic dipole moment $M_{\text{dipole}}$ of the entire hollow cone, we integrate $dM$ over the entire slant length from $l = 0$ to $l = L_{\text{slant}}$:

$$M_{\text{dipole}} = \int\limits_{0}^{L_{\text{slant}}} \frac{Q\omega \sin^2 \alpha}{L_{\text{slant}}^2} \, l^3 \, dl$$

$$M_{\text{dipole}} = \frac{Q\omega \sin^2 \alpha}{L_{\text{slant}}^2} \int\limits_{0}^{L_{\text{slant}}} l^3 \, dl$$

$$M_{\text{dipole}} = \frac{Q\omega \sin^2 \alpha}{L_{\text{slant}}^2} \left[\frac{l^4}{4}\right]_{0}^{L_{\text{slant}}}$$

$$M_{\text{dipole}} = \frac{Q\omega \sin^2 \alpha}{L_{\text{slant}}^2} \times \frac{L_{\text{slant}}^4}{4} = \frac{1}{4} Q\omega \sin^2 \alpha \times L_{\text{slant}}^2$$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { As } \sin \alpha=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{~L}_{\text {slant }}} \Rightarrow \sin ^2 \alpha \times \mathrm{L}_{\text {slant }}^2=\mathrm{R}^2 . \\ & \qquad \mathrm{M}_{\text {dipole }}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{Q} \omega \mathrm{R}^2 \end{aligned} $$

Using expression for the far-field magnetic dipole along the axis:

$\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 \mathrm{M}_{\text {dipole }}}{\mathrm{z}^3}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2\left(\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{Q} \omega \mathrm{R}^2\right)}{\mathrm{z}^3}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{QR}^2 \omega}{\mathrm{z}^3}=0.5 \frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{QR}^2 \omega}{\mathrm{z}^3}$

$$ \Rightarrow 0.5 \frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{QR}^2 \omega}{\mathrm{z}^3}=\mathrm{n} \frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{QR}^2 \omega}{\mathrm{z}^3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=0.5 $$

Therefore, the correct answer is 0.5 .
title: JEE Advanced 2026 Paper 1 Online

subject: Physics
solution:
For a single circular turn of radius $r$ carrying current $I$, the magnetic moment is :

$$ \mathrm{m}=\mathrm{I} \cdot \mathrm{~A}=\mathrm{I}\left(\pi \mathrm{r}^2\right) $$

In a flat coil where the turns are wound from $\mathrm{r}_1$ to $\mathrm{r}_2$, the number of turns per unit radial length is $\mathrm{n}=\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{r}_2-\mathrm{r}_1}$. The total magnetic moment ( M ) is the integral of the moments of all infinitesimal turns of thickness dr :

$$ M=\int\limits_{r_1}^{r_2}\left(I \pi r^2\right) \cdot n d r $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{M}=\mathrm{I} \pi \mathrm{n} \int\limits_{\mathrm{r}_1}^{\mathrm{r}_2} \mathrm{r}^2 \mathrm{dr}=\mathrm{I} \pi\left(\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{r}_2-\mathrm{r}_1}\right)\left[\frac{\mathrm{r}^3}{3}\right]_{\mathrm{r}_1}^{\mathrm{r}_2}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{M}=\frac{\mathrm{NI} \pi}{3\left(\mathrm{r}_2-\mathrm{r}_1\right)}\left(\mathrm{r}_2^3-\mathrm{r}_1^3\right) $$

Using the algebraic identity $r_2^3-r_1^3=\left(r_2-r_1\right)\left(r_2^2+r_1^2+r_1 r_2\right)$ :

$$ \mathrm{M}=\frac{\mathrm{NI} \pi}{3}\left(\mathrm{r}_1^2+\mathrm{r}_2^2+\mathrm{r}_1 \mathrm{r}_2\right) $$

Substituting the values, where number of turns is $\mathrm{N}=200$, the current is $\mathrm{I}=20 \mathrm{~mA}=20 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}$, the inner radius of coil is $\mathrm{r}_1=3 \mathrm{~cm}=0.03 \mathrm{~m}$ and the outer radius is $\mathrm{r}_2=6 \mathrm{~cm}=0.06 \mathrm{~m}$

$$ \mathrm{M}=\frac{200 \times 20 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.14}{3}\left(0.03^2+0.06^2+0.03 \times 0.06\right) $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $M=\frac{4 \times 3.14}{3}(0.0009+0.0036+0.0018)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{M}=\frac{12.56}{3}(0.0063)=12.56 \times 0.0021$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ M=0.026376 \approx 2.64 \times 10^{-2} A . m^2=\alpha \times 10^{-2} A . m^2 \Rightarrow \alpha=2.64 $$

Therefore, the value of $\alpha$ is 2.64 .

Thus, the correct option is (B).
title: JEE Main 2026 (Online) 4th April Morning Shift

subject: Physics
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{dM}=\mathrm{dIA} \\ & \mathrm{A}=\pi \mathrm{r}^2=\pi(\mathrm{R} \sin \theta)^2 \\ & \mathrm{dI}=\frac{\mathrm{da}}{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{\sigma(2 \pi \mathrm{r})(\mathrm{R} \mathrm{d} \theta) \omega}{2 \pi} \\ & \mathrm{dI}=\frac{\sigma 2 \pi \mathrm{R}^2 \omega \sin \theta \mathrm{~d} \theta}{2 \pi}\end{aligned}$

$$ \mathrm{dI}=\sigma \mathrm{R}^2 \omega \sin \theta \mathrm{~d} \theta $$

Magnetic dipole moment :

$$ \begin{aligned} & M=\int d M=\int_0^\pi \sigma R^2 \omega \pi R^2 \sin ^3 \theta d \theta \\ & M=\sigma R^4 \omega \pi \int_0^\pi \sin ^3 \theta d \theta \quad\left(\because \int_0^\pi \sin ^3 \theta d \theta=\frac{4}{3}\right) \\ & M=\left(\frac{Q}{4 \pi R^2}\right) R^4 \omega \pi\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \end{aligned} $$

Magnetic dipole moment

$$ \mathrm{M}=\frac{\mathrm{QR}^2 \omega}{3} $$

Angular momentum

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{L}=\left(\frac{2}{5} \mathrm{MR}^2\right) \omega \\ & \frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{~L}}=\frac{\mathrm{QR}^2 \omega}{3 \times \frac{2}{5} \mathrm{MR}^2 \omega}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2 \mathrm{M}}\left(\frac{5}{3}\right) \end{aligned} $$

$\alpha=\frac{5}{3}=1.67$
title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 2 Online

subject: Physics
solution:
To find the work done in turning the coil through $$90^{\circ}$$, we first need to understand the concept of torque on a current-carrying loop in a magnetic field and how work done relates to the change in potential energy of the system. The potential energy (U) of a magnetic dipole in a magnetic field is given by:

$$U = - \vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}$$

where:

  • $\vec{M}$ is the magnetic moment of the coil, and
  • $\vec{B}$ is the magnetic field.

For a coil with $N$ turns, carrying current $I$, and with an area $A$, the magnetic moment $\vec{M}$ is defined as:

$$M = NI \cdot A$$

Given that the coil has $$100$$ turns, carries a current of $$1 \, \mathrm{mA} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{A}$$, and the area of the coil is $$5 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{m}^2$$, we can calculate its magnetic moment as follows:

$$M = 100 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{Am}^2$$

Since the coil is initially placed such that its plane is perpendicular to the magnetic field (i.e., the angle $ \theta = 0^{\circ} $), and then it is turned through $90^{\circ}$, the initial and final angles ($\theta_i$ and $\theta_f$) of the coil with respect to the magnetic field are $0^{\circ}$ and $90^{\circ}$ respectively. This means the initial potential energy ($U_i$) and final potential energy ($U_f$) of the system are:

$$U_i = - M B \cos(\theta_i)$$

$$U_f = - M B \cos(\theta_f)$$

Given that $B = 0.20 \, \mathrm{T}$, $\theta_i = 0^{\circ} \, (\cos(0) = 1)$, and $\theta_f = 90^{\circ} \, (\cos(90^{\circ}) = 0)$, the potential energies are:

$$U_i = - 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.20 \cdot 1 = - 1 \times 10^{-4} \, \mathrm{J}$$

$$U_f = - 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.20 \cdot 0 = 0 \, \mathrm{J}$$

The work done ($W$) is equal to the change in potential energy:

$$W = U_f - U_i$$

$$W = 0 - (- 1 \times 10^{-4}) = 1 \times 10^{-4} \, \mathrm{J}$$

Therefore, the work done in turning the coil through $90^{\circ}$ is $$100 \mu \mathrm{J}$$.
title: JEE Main 2024 (Online) 6th April Evening Shift

subject: Physics
solution: $$\begin{aligned} & W=U_f-U_i=(-M B \cos 90)-(-M B \cos 0) \\ & \Rightarrow W=M B=N i A B=5 \mu \mathrm{J} \end{aligned}$$
title: JEE Main 2024 (Online) 6th April Morning Shift

subject: Physics
solution:
Magnetic moment is defined as the product of the magnet's pole strength and the distance between the poles (also known as the magnetic length). When a magnetic strip is bent, its magnetic moment changes based on the new configuration.

Consider a straight magnetic strip with a magnetic moment of $$44 \, \text{Am}^2$$. If this strip is bent into a semicircular shape, we need to find the new effective magnetic moment.

The magnetic moment in a straight strip is given by:

$$ M_{\text{straight}} = m \cdot l $$

where:

  • $$m$$ is the pole strength
  • $$l$$ is the magnetic length

Given $$M_{\text{straight}} = 44 \, \text{Am}^2$$, let's now consider the strip bent into a semicircular shape.

When the strip is bent into a semicircle, the effective distance between the magnetic poles is the diameter of the semicircle. Let's denote the original length of the strip as $$L$$. In a straight line, this length $$L$$ is also the magnetic length. When bent into a semicircle, the length of the arc of the semicircle is still $$L$$.

The circumference of a full circle is given by:

$$ C = 2\pi R $$

Therefore, the length of the arc of a semicircle is:

$$ L = \pi R $$

Solving for $$R$$, we get:

$$ R = \frac{L}{\pi} $$

The diameter of the semicircle (which is the new effective magnetic length, $$l_{\text{new}}$$) is twice the radius:

$$ l_{\text{new}} = 2R = 2 \cdot \frac{L}{\pi} = \frac{2L}{\pi} $$

Now, the new magnetic moment $$M_{\text{new}}$$ is:

$$ M_{\text{new}} = m \cdot l_{\text{new}} = m \cdot \frac{2L}{\pi} $$

We know from the original magnetic strip:

$$ M_{\text{straight}} = m \cdot L = 44 \, \text{Am}^2 $$

Rewriting $$m$$ in terms of the known magnetic moment of the straight strip:

$$ m = \frac{44}{L} $$

Substituting $$m$$ into the new magnetic moment equation:

$$ M_{\text{new}} = \frac{44}{L} \cdot \frac{2L}{\pi} $$

Canceling out $$L$$ from the numerator and the denominator:

$$ M_{\text{new}} = \frac{44 \cdot 2}{\pi} = \frac{88}{\pi} $$

Given $$\pi = \frac{22}{7}$$, we substitute this value into the equation:

$$ M_{\text{new}} = \frac{88 \cdot 7}{22} = 28 \, \text{Am}^2 $$

Therefore, the magnetic moment of the strip when bent into a semicircular shape is $$28 \, \text{Am}^2$$.

Ui + Ki = Uf + Kf

subject: Physics
solution:
Magnetic dipole moment

M = iA

$$ \Rightarrow $$ M = $$\left( {{q \over T}} \right) \times \pi {R^2}$$

= $${{{q\pi {R^2}} \over {\left( {{{2\pi r} \over v}} \right)}}}$$ = $${{qvR} \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ M = $${{qv} \over 2} \times {{vm} \over {qB}}$$

As you can see from the figure, direction of magnetic moment(M) is opposite to magnetic field.

$$ \therefore $$ $$\overrightarrow M = - {{m{v^2}} \over {2B}}\widehat B$$

= $$ - {{m{v^2}} \over {2B}}\left( {{{\overrightarrow B } \over B}} \right)$$

= $$ - {{m{v^2}} \over {2{B^2}}}\overrightarrow B $$
title: JEE Main 2020 (Online) 6th September Evening Slot

subject: Physics
solution:
Let the given square loop has side $a$, then its magnetic dipole moment will be

$$ m=I a^2 $$

When square is converted into a circular loop of radius $r$,


Then, wire length will be same in both area,

$$ \Rightarrow 4 a=2 \pi r \Rightarrow r=\frac{4 a}{2 \pi}=\frac{2 a}{\pi} $$

Hence, area of circular loop formed is, $A^{\prime}=\pi r^2$

$$ =\pi\left(\frac{2 a}{\pi}\right)^2=\frac{4 a^2}{\pi} $$

Magnitude of magnetic dipole moment of circular loop will be

$$ m^{\prime}=I A^{\prime}=I \frac{4 a^2}{\pi} $$

Ratio of magnetic dipole moments of both shapes is,

$$ \begin{aligned} \frac{m^{\prime}}{m}=\frac{I \cdot \frac{4 a^2}{\pi}}{I a^2}=\frac{4}{\pi} \\\\ \Rightarrow m^{\prime} =\frac{4 m}{\pi}(\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{m}) \end{aligned} $$

Then, torque on the current coil is

$$\tau = m \times B = mB\sin 90^\circ = NIAB$$

$$ = NI\pi {r^2}B(N - m)$$

subject: Physics
solution:
Let the point charge Q is moving in a circle of constant radius R in anticlockwise direction as shown in the figure.

The magnetic flux through the circular loop at time t is given by $$\phi = B(\overrightarrow t )\,.\,(\pi {R^2}\widehat z)\, = \pi B(t){R^2}$$. Faraday's law gives the induced emf e as

$$e = - {{d\phi } \over {dt}} = - \pi {R^2}{{dB(t)} \over {dt}} = - \pi {R^2}B$$ ..... (1)

Lenz's law gives the direction of induced current and hence electric field $$(\overrightarrow E )$$ as clockwise. The induced emf is related to $$\overrightarrow E $$ by

$$e = \oint {\overrightarrow E .\,d\overrightarrow l = - E(2\pi R)} $$ ..... (2)

Eliminate e from equations (1) and (2) to get E = BR/2.
title: JEE Advanced 2013 Paper 2 Offline

subject: Physics
solution:
$${M \over L} = {Q \over {2m}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$M = \left( {{Q \over {2m}}} \right)L$$

$$ \Rightarrow M \propto L$$, where $$\gamma = {Q \over {2m}}$$

$$\left( {{Q \over {2m}}} \right)(I\omega )$$

$$ = \left( {{Q \over {2m}}} \right)(m{R^2}\omega ) = {{Q\omega {R^2}} \over 2}$$

Induced electric field is opposite. Therefore,

$$\omega ' = \omega - \alpha t$$

$$\alpha = {\tau \over I} = {{(QE)R} \over {m{R^2}}} = {{(Q)\left( {{{BR} \over 2}} \right)R} \over {m{R^2}}} = {{QB} \over {2m}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\omega ' = \omega - {{QB} \over {2m}}.1 = \omega - {{QB} \over {2m}}$$

$${M_f} = {{Q\omega '{R^2}} \over 2} = Q\left( {\omega - {{QB} \over {2m}}} \right){{{R^2}} \over 2}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\Delta M = {M_f} - {M_i} = - {{{Q^2}B{R^2}} \over {4m}}$$

$$M = - \gamma {{QB{R^2}} \over 2}$$ (as $$\gamma = {Q \over {2m}}$$)
title: JEE Advanced 2013 Paper 2 Offline

subject: Physics
solution:
Area of the loop

$$A = \left[ {{a^2} + 4 \times {{\pi {{\left( {{a \over 2}} \right)}^2}} \over 2}} \right]\widehat k = \left[ {{a^2} + {{\pi {a^2}} \over 2}} \right]\widehat k$$

Therefore, the magnetic moment of the current loop is

$$M = I \times A = I\left[ {{a^2} + {{\pi {a^2}} \over 2}} \right]\widehat k = \left[ {1 + {\pi \over 2}} \right]I{a^2}\widehat k$$
title: IIT-JEE 2012 Paper 2 Offline

subject: Physics
solution:
(A) Any static charge produces electric field $\forall$ invariant with time. Uniformly charged dielectric does the same.

(B) Rotating charged behaves like a current. hence, magnetic field is produced around the ring and also if there is current in rotating ring, ring has magnetic moment.

(C) Since, net charge is zero there will be no time independent electric field. The current produces magnetic field and magnetic moment.

(D) A changing magnetic field will be produced. This will create a induced electric field.

Also a changing magnetic moment will be produced.
title: IIT-JEE 2006
